title: Project: Terminal Map
summary: Build a small folder map for a park survey team by creating and checking a nested workspace with pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, and touch.

sketchBlocks.title: Plan a folder map before you build it
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A good terminal project starts with a simple real-world goal. Imagine you are helping a park survey team organize notes for three trail areas. Your job is to create a clear folder map so the team can store files in the right places.

In this topic, the final deliverable is a small workspace structure for a **park-survey** project. You will create folders and note files, then inspect them to confirm the map is correct.

A command like `mkdir -p` is useful when you want to create nested folders in one step.

```bash
mkdir -p park-survey/trails/north
```

Line by line:

- `mkdir` means create folder.
- `-p` allows Linux to create parent folders that do not exist yet.
- `park-survey/trails/north` is the full path of nested folders to build.

After running that command, the workspace includes a top folder named `park-survey`, a folder inside it named `trails`, and another inside that named `north`.

This project uses that same idea to build a small, useful folder map instead of creating folders one at a time.

sketchBlocks.title: Move into the right place and verify it
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Creating folders is only part of the job. You also need to move into the correct location and confirm where you are.

Two beginner-friendly commands help here:

```bash
cd park-survey
pwd
ls
```

Step by step:

- `cd park-survey` moves you into the `park-survey` folder.
- `pwd` prints your current location so you can verify you moved successfully.
- `ls` lists what is inside the current folder.

If `park-survey` contains a folder named `trails`, then `ls` should show `trails` while you are inside `park-survey`.

This matters in a project because creating a file in the wrong folder can break the whole map. Checking with `pwd` and `ls` helps you catch mistakes early.

sketchBlocks.title: Add files that make the map useful
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A folder map becomes more useful when it includes files that show what each area is for. In this project, you will add note files for trail areas so the survey team has clear placeholders for future observations.

You can create empty files with `touch`.

```bash
cd park-survey/trails
touch north.txt south.txt east.txt
ls
```

Line by line:

- `cd park-survey/trails` moves into the `trails` folder.
- `touch north.txt south.txt east.txt` creates three empty files.
- `ls` confirms the files are there.

By the end of the project, your workspace will show both structure and purpose: folders for organization and files for the team to fill in later.

quizDraft.title: Create the park survey folder map
quizDraft.prompt: You are the junior organizer for a park survey team. Build the first version of the team's workspace so they can sort trail notes by area. In the lesson workspace, create a folder named `park-survey`. Inside it, create a folder named `trails`. Inside `trails`, create three folders named `north`, `south`, and `east`. Use the terminal to complete the task, then check that the file explorer shows the nested map.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Create the top folder first or use one command that can build nested folders.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can create a nested folder structure in the workspace using Linux folder commands.
help.hint_1: You need one top-level folder, one folder inside it, and three area folders inside `trails`.
help.hint_2: A command with `mkdir -p` can create `park-survey/trails/...` paths without stopping at each level.

quizDraft.title: Add trail note files to the map
quizDraft.prompt: Continue the same park survey project from the previous step. The folder map already exists. Now add placeholder note files for the survey team. Inside `park-survey/trails`, create three files named `north.txt`, `south.txt`, and `east.txt`. Keep the existing folders from the previous step, and do not remove anything.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: This step starts from the working folder map you already built.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can continue a project from an existing workspace and add files in the correct folder without rebuilding from scratch incorrectly.
help.hint_1: First move into the folder that should contain the three note files, then create the files there.
help.hint_2: The files belong directly inside `park-survey/trails`, while the `north`, `south`, and `east` folders should still remain in place.

quizDraft.title: Finish and verify the survey workspace
quizDraft.prompt: Continue the same park survey project from the previous step. The folders and trail note files already exist. Add one more file named `README.txt` inside `park-survey` so the team has a top-level project note. Then move into `park-survey`, run commands that confirm your location and show its contents, and leave the finished workspace ready for review.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: This step should keep everything from the earlier steps and add one top-level file.

help.concept: This task checks whether you can extend an existing workspace, place a file at the correct level, and verify the result with navigation and inspection commands.
help.hint_1: `README.txt` belongs inside `park-survey`, not inside `trails`. After creating it, move into `park-survey`.
help.hint_2: Use one command to show your current location and another to list what is inside `park-survey` after you enter it.
